questions.specification:
Decoupling is a central aim of sustainable development, measuring the relationship between economic growth and its associated environmental pressures.

The table shows changes over a 5-year period:

| Indicator | Change over 5 years |
|---|---|
| GDP | +15% |
| CO2 emissions | -5% |

Which type of decoupling is illustrated by this data?

options.markscheme: Relative decoupling refers to a situation where the environmental impact is still increasing, but at a lower rate than the economic growth.
Absolute decoupling occurs when the economic indicator (GDP) increases while the environmental pressure (CO2 emissions) decreases, as shown in the table.
Dematerialization is the reduction of total material and energy throughput of any product or service, rather than the macro-economic relationship between growth and impact.
Product stewardship is a policy approach where producers take responsibility for the entire life cycle of a product, which is not what the data illustrates.

questions.specification:
A city library is replacing its aging public seating with a new modular bench system for indoor and sheltered outdoor areas. The design team has created an online configurator where the library can choose:

- module length (single, double, triple)
- seat material (recycled plastic slats, FSC-certified timber, powder-coated aluminium)
- add-ons (arm rests, removable cushions, signage panel)

The configurator displays an estimated environmental profile for each configuration (carbon footprint per seat over 10 years, percentage recycled content, and an end-of-life plan: reuse, remanufacture, or recycling). The library must justify its final selection to the city council using sustainable development principles.

Figure 1 and Figure 2 show the configurator and a summary page.

![A screenshot of a "Modular Bench Configurator" page for a city library. A progress bar shows steps: Choose location, Select modules, Pick materials, Add features, Review. A panel shows estimated 10-year CO2e per seat, recycled content percentage, and end-of-life option. A "Next: Pick materials" button is highlighted.](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/98e2d5f8-acfa-41da-8ea4-88f34d702f3e.png)

Figure 1: Configurator page for selecting modules and materials

![A screenshot of a review/summary page listing a base bench configuration and selected add-ons. A table shows the chosen material, recycled content, expected service life, repairability score, and an end-of-life route (reuse/remanufacture/recycle). A total "estimated 10-year CO2e per seat" value is shown at the bottom.](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/2ffa6c3c-e30c-41f6-ab17-4747a18558f3.png)

Figure 2: Summary page showing the selected configuration and environmental profile

parts.content: State two ways sustainable development considers more than one type of impact when making decisions about products.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognises environmental impacts (e.g., resource use, emissions, pollution, biodiversity) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Recognises social impacts (e.g., health, safety, equity, user wellbeing, community) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Recognises economic impacts (e.g., affordability, jobs, long-term cost/value) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 2 marks</note>

parts.content: Outline how the configurator summary (Figure 2) helps the library make a sustainable purchasing decision.
parts.markscheme:
- Makes lifecycle impacts visible (e.g., showing $\mathrm{CO_2e}$ over 10 years rather than only purchase choice) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enables comparison of options using sustainability indicators (e.g., recycled content/repairability/end-of-life route) to select a lower-impact configuration <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline how choosing a modular bench system supports at least one principle of sustainable development.
parts.markscheme:
- Modular design enables repair/upgrade/part replacement so the product lasts longer and reduces material demand <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Modules can be reused/remanufactured at end-of-life to keep materials in circulation (circular economy) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Standardised modules can reduce waste by fitting different spaces without bespoke one-off manufacture <marks>1 mark</marks>
<note>Max 2 marks</note>

parts.content: Explain how design guidance could be applied to ensure the benches remain sustainable throughout their life cycle.
parts.markscheme:
- Specify materials with lower impact and responsible sourcing (e.g., FSC timber, high recycled content, low-toxicity finishes) and justify via intended use/location <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Design for durability in the library context (e.g., abrasion/UV/moisture resistance, robust joints/fasteners) to reduce replacement frequency <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Design for disassembly and repair (e.g., mechanical fixings, accessible fasteners, replaceable slats/cushions) to support maintenance/remanufacture <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Plan end-of-life using clear routes (reuse/remanufacture/recycle), including material separation/marking and take-back instructions for the supplier <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: Which of the following best describes the concept of sustainable development?

options.content: Using resources at a rate that meets current needs without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs
Maximizing the use of renewable resources to meet current needs
Minimizing the use of non-renewable resources regardless of current needs
Focusing on immediate economic benefits without considering long-term environmental impact

questions.specification:
An industrialized nation recorded the following economic and environmental data over a five-year period:

| Year | Gross Domestic Product (GDP) | Total Carbon Emissions |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 2018 | $1.8 Trillion | 540 Million Tonnes |
| 2023 | $2.1 Trillion | 515 Million Tonnes |

Which sustainable development concept is illustrated by the relationship between these two variables?

options.markscheme: Relative decoupling occurs when the environmental impact grows at a slower rate than the economic indicator; here, the impact is actually decreasing.
Absolute decoupling is defined as the situation where the economy grows (GDP increases from $1.8T to $2.1T) while the environmental impact declines (emissions decrease from 540M to 515M tonnes).
This refers to a corporate accounting framework that includes social, environmental, and financial components, rather than the macro-economic relationship shown here.
Resource leveling is a project management technique used to address over-allocation of resources, not a sustainable development metric.

questions.specification:
A furniture manufacturer released the following environmental and economic performance data for two consecutive fiscal years:

| Performance Metric | Year 1 | Year 2 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Gross Profit | $2.4 million | $2.8 million |
| Manufacturing Timber Waste | 620 tonnes | 585 tonnes |

Which sustainability concept is demonstrated by the trend in the manufacturer’s data?
Which of the following is a principle of sustainable development?

options.content: Meeting present needs without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs
Maximizing economic growth regardless of environmental impact
Focusing solely on the use of renewable resources
Prioritizing immediate benefits over long-term sustainability

questions.specification:
A city council is replacing 20,000 streetlights. It is considering a new luminaire system called **LumaLoop**. The supplier proposes:

- Replacing existing high-pressure sodium lamps with LED luminaires.
- Using housings made from at least 70% recycled aluminium and a single polymer lens marked for recycling.
- Designing the unit so the LED module, driver and lens can be replaced with basic tools (no glue), and providing a 12-year repair-spares agreement.
- Adding a QR code on each column that links to a public dashboard showing energy use, estimated CO$_2$e savings, repair rates and end-of-life returns.
- A take-back scheme where old luminaires are collected, sorted and sent either to refurbishment for reuse or to material recycling.

A pilot trial on one road found:

| Item | Existing lights (per year) | LumaLoop pilot (per year) |
|---|---:|---:|
| Electricity use per light | 420 kWh | 135 kWh |
| Reported night-time collisions on the road | 14 | 11 |
| Resident complaints about glare | 6 | 9 |
| Unit purchase cost | £0 (already owned) | £165 |
| Estimated maintenance visits per light | 0.40 | 0.18 |

Some residents support the change due to lower energy bills and carbon emissions. Others worry about light pollution and the environmental impact of disposing of the old lights.

![night-time streetlight trial photo with LED lamps on one side of a road and older orange lamps on the other](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/e445f089-5b8e-4808-aac3-5acfb599d10d.png)

Figure 1: Trial installation comparing light colour and distribution

![dashboard-style label showing kWh, CO2e, repair rate and take-back return rate with a QR code](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/bfa609d6-ab79-4751-8d6a-d9f862f52305.png)

Figure 2: Example of the public performance dashboard label

parts.content: List two features of the LumaLoop proposal that support sustainable development.
parts.markscheme:
- Identifies a valid feature linked to sustainable development (e.g., lower energy use/CO$_2$e, recycled aluminium, repairability, take-back scheme, public reporting). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identifies a second valid feature linked to sustainable development. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline how publishing the QR-linked dashboard could improve stakeholder trust in the project.
parts.markscheme:
- Makes a clear point about transparency/accountability (e.g., open reporting of energy, emissions, repairs, returns). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Links this to improved trust/credibility and reduced greenwashing concerns for residents/taxpayers. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline how the LumaLoop luminaire applies one principle of sustainable development through its design guidance.
parts.markscheme:
- Names/identifies an appropriate sustainable development principle (e.g., resource efficiency, waste minimisation/circularity, long-term thinking, precautionary principle, stewardship). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Applies it to a specific design guidance decision (e.g., modular replaceable parts, material selection for recyclability, take-back/repair spares). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how changing to LumaLoop could affect sustainable development outcomes for the city, using evidence from the pilot data.
parts.markscheme:
- Uses pilot evidence to explain an environmental impact/benefit (e.g., lower kWh reduces emissions). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Uses pilot evidence to explain an economic impact/benefit/cost (e.g., purchase cost vs reduced maintenance visits). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Uses pilot evidence to explain a social impact/benefit/issue (e.g., collisions down, glare complaints up). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains a trade-off and why it matters for decision-making (e.g., safety vs light pollution/glare; upfront cost vs lifecycle cost). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Makes a justified overall judgement about the likely net outcome for sustainable development, supported by the evidence given. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 5 marks</note>

parts.content: Explain how design guidance could be used to reduce the negative impacts identified in the trial while maintaining the benefits, in line with sustainable development.
parts.markscheme:
- Identifies a negative impact/concern from the case (e.g., glare complaints, light pollution, disposal of old units). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Proposes a relevant design guidance modification for lighting quality (e.g., optics, shielding, colour temperature choice, dimming/controls). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains how the modification reduces the negative impact (e.g., less glare/skyglow, improved visual comfort). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Proposes a relevant design guidance approach for end-of-life (e.g., design for disassembly, materials marking, take-back logistics, refurbishment pathway). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains how this supports circularity/resource stewardship (e.g., reuse/refurbish first, recycle second; reduced virgin material demand). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Considers stakeholder needs (e.g., residents, maintenance teams, council budget) when justifying choices. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Links decisions to long-term outcomes (e.g., lifecycle cost, resilience, reduced emissions over time). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identifies a measurable KPI that could be tracked via the dashboard to verify improvement (e.g., glare complaints per month, return rate, energy per light, repair rate). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provides a coherent, sustainable-development-aligned conclusion balancing environmental, social and economic dimensions. <marks>1 mark</marks>
<note>Max 9 marks</note>
